
<!-- rnb-text-begin -->

---
title: "R Notebook"
output: html_notebook
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

@inproceedings{johnson-etal-2021-classical, title = "The {C}lassical
{L}anguage {T}oolkit: {A}n {NLP} Framework for Pre-Modern Languages",
author = "Johnson, Kyle P. and Burns, Patrick J. and Stewart, John and
Cook, Todd and Besnier, Cl{'e}ment and Mattingly, William J. B.",
booktitle = "Proceedings of the 59th Annual Meeting of the Association
for Computational Linguistics and the 11th International Joint
Conference on Natural Language Processing: System Demonstrations", month
= aug, year = "2021", address = "Online", publisher = "Association for
Computational Linguistics", url =
"<https://aclanthology.org/2021.acl-demo.3>", doi =
"10.18653/v1/2021.acl-demo.3", pages = "20--29", abstract = "This paper
announces version 1.0 of the Classical Language Toolkit (CLTK), an NLP
framework for pre-modern languages. The vast majority of NLP, its
algorithms and software, is created with assumptions particular to
living languages, thus neglecting certain important characteristics of
largely non-spoken historical languages. Further, scholars of pre-modern
languages often have different goals than those of living-language
researchers. To fill this void, the CLTK adapts ideas from several
leading NLP frameworks to create a novel software architecture that
satisfies the unique needs of pre-modern languages and their
researchers. Its centerpiece is a modular processing pipeline that
balances the competing demands of algorithmic diversity with
pre-configured defaults. The CLTK currently provides pipelines,
including models, for almost 20 languages.", }

This is an [R Markdown](http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com) Notebook. When you
execute code within the notebook, the results appear beneath the code.

Try executing this chunk by clicking the *Run* button within the chunk
or by placing your cursor inside it and pressing *Ctrl+Shift+Enter*.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->

<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxucGxvdChjYXJzKVxuYGBgIn0= -->

```r
plot(cars)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbXX0= -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


*Add a new chunk by clicking the* Insert Chunk\* button on the toolbar
or by pressing *Ctrl+Alt+I*.

When you save the notebook, an HTML file containing the code and output
will be saved alongside it (click the *Preview* button or press
*Ctrl+Shift+K* to preview the HTML file).\*

*The preview shows you a rendered HTML copy of the contents of the
editor. Consequently, unlike* Knit*,* Preview\* does not run any R code
chunks. Instead, the output of the chunk when it was last run in the
editor is displayed.\*

# Data Preparation


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

# install.packages("factoextra")
# install.packages("dplyr")
# install.packages("ggrepel")
# install.packages("tidyverse")

library(factoextra)
library(dplyr)
library(ggrepel)
library(tidyverse)
library(tidytable)
library(cluster)
library(combinat)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


(For the above ggrepel library, see <https://github.com/slowkow/ggrepel>


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r
setwd("~/GitHub/cltk-2025-atticus")

dims <- c("title", "author", "path", "form", "lemma", "tag", "Aspect", "Mood", "Number",
                        "Person",
                        "Tense",
                        "VerbForm",
                        "Voice",
                        "Case",
                        "PronType",
                        "Gender",
                        "Polarity",
                        "Degree",
                        "NumType")

data <- read.csv("atticus-study-results.csv", na.strings="", col.names = dims, stringsAsFactors = TRUE)

summary(data)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->

```
                 title                            author       
 Ab urbe condita    : 935517   M. Tullius Cicero     :1172096  
 Naturalis Historia : 424090   Titus Livius (Livy)   : 935444  
 Institutio Oratoria: 181915   Pliny the Elder       : 424090  
 Letters to Atticus : 138923   Seneca, Lucius Annaeus: 343661  
 Metamorphoses      : 137269   P. Ovidius Naso       : 231717  
 Noctes Atticae     : 135858   (Other)               :2072353  
 (Other)            :3337242   NA's                  : 111453  
                                                                                                         path             
 ..\\canonical-latinLit\\data\\phi0914\\phi001\\phi0914.phi001.perseus-lat2.xml                                 : 537992  
 C:\\Users\\T470s\\Documents\\GitHub\\canonical-latinLit\\data\\phi0978\\phi001\\phi0978.phi001.perseus-lat2.xml: 424090  
 C:\\Users\\T470s\\Documents\\GitHub\\canonical-latinLit\\data\\phi1002\\phi001\\phi1002.phi001.perseus-lat2.xml: 181915  
 ..\\canonical-latinLit\\data\\phi0474\\phi057\\phi0474.phi057.perseus-lat1.xml                                 : 138923  
 C:\\Users\\T470s\\Documents\\GitHub\\canonical-latinLit\\data\\phi1254\\phi001\\phi1254.phi001.perseus-lat2.xml: 135858  
 (Other)                                                                                                        :3872015  
 NA's                                                                                                           :     21  
      form             lemma              tag            Aspect          Mood          Number            Person       
 .      : 235838   .      : 235838   NOUN   :1401399   2    :     27   Fin :     13   Act :     12   3      : 649359  
 et     : 129866   sum    : 167498   VERB   :1074985   Fut  :      1   Imp :  24664   Fin :     27   2      : 153310  
 in     :  97910   et     : 132682   ADJ    : 421235   Imp  : 817276   Ind : 581518   Pass:      2   1      : 110542  
 est    :  58667   qui    : 120182   DET    : 334561   Perf : 381236   Part:      1   Plur:1028908   Act    :     27  
 non    :  52563   in     :  99386   PRON   : 331611   Pres :     12   Pres:     27   Sing:2518333   Nom    :     14  
 (Other):4715937   (Other):4535216   (Other):1726991   Prosp:  42438   Sub : 139450   NA's:1743532   (Other):      7  
 NA's   :     33   NA's   :     12   NA's   :     32   NA's :4049824   NA's:4545141                  NA's   :4377555  
  Tense         VerbForm        Voice              Case            PronType        Gender        Polarity      
 Abl :      1   Fem :      2   Act : 706074   Nom    : 968955   Prs    : 227796   Fem : 844030   Neg :  85984  
 Fut :  32899   Fin : 746898   Masc:      1   Acc    : 787353   Rel    : 222358   Masc:1196823   NA's:5204830  
 Past: 226162   Inf : 169980   Pass: 368911   Abl    : 646477   Dem    : 155576   Neut: 678978                 
 Pqp :  15084   Masc:     17   NA's:4215828   Gen    : 309747   Ind    :  81269   NA's:2570983                 
 Pres: 471155   Neut:      2                  Dat    : 107229   Tot    :  35500                                
 NA's:4545513   Part: 324279                  (Other):   2136   (Other):  56237                                
                NA's:4049636                  NA's   :2468917   NA's   :4512078                                
  Degree        NumType       
 Abs :  31300   Card:  49644  
 Cmp :  52171   Dist:    888  
 NA's:5207343   Ord :  14786  
                NA's:5225496  
                              
                              
                              
```



<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


One thing I want to note is that not every value is relevant. I'll do
this further below and not here, but I want to go one at a time and see
whether Degree [c("Abs", "Cmp")], Gender [c("Fem", "Masc", "Neut")], or
Case (c("Nom", "Acc", "Abl", "Gen", "Dat", "Loc", "Voc", "Abs")) make
any difference. Some potential issues are also that there are
redundancies in the variables. If we want to do significance testing, we
want to know each variable is independent. So, if we get rid of POS
tags, we should still keep SCONJ, ADP, CCONJ,


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r
#Lets get rid of the non-Cicero works now, but save the Cicero works for comparison
data.with.cicero <- data
data <- data[rowSums(data == 'M. Tullius Cicero', na.rm = TRUE) == 0 | data$title == "Letters to Atticus",]

titles <- unique(data$title)
num_words <- table(data$title)
final.frame <- data.frame(title=titles)

```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Now that we have the data, what we're *really* interested in is how many
of each tag appears in each work (how many of each POS, how many perfect
verbs, whatever). The point is, we're not interested in every individual
word.

This block of code will loop through and create a new data frame called
***final.frame*** (which is probably poorly named, but I'll fix it later
or I won't). This data frame has a variable for the count of each tag,
and every row is a unique work.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

for (i in 1:length(titles)) {
  frame <- data[data$title == titles[i],]
  frame <- subset(frame, select= c(-form, -path, -NumType, -author, -lemma))
  for (n in 2:length(frame)) {
    t <- table(frame[,n])
    #t <- prop.table(t)
    names <- names(t)
    
    for (name_index in seq(1, length(names))) {
      final.frame[i, names[name_index]] <- unname(t[names[name_index]])
    }
  }
}

#Normalize by number of words in each work
for (i in 2:length(final.frame[1,])) {
  for (n in 1:length(final.frame[,1])) {
    final.frame[n,i] <- final.frame[n,i] * (10000 / num_words[[final.frame[n,]$title]])
  }
}
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


A few things will make our life easier. We don't want any NAs in there,
because that'll gum up the clustering. We'll replace any missing values
with 0, because 0 occurrences of a variable is the only reason there
would be NA values at this stage.

I have a *hopefully* temporary measure where I replace row 46's title
with Apocolocynthosis. The title is given as "Petronius. Seneca
Apocolocynthosis" because it's based off a Loeb edition of that name.
However, when I abbreviate the names at the end of this block, this
makes it ambiguous with the other Perseus text which contains just
Petronius, and **rownames()** doesn't allow duplicates.

I also remove Cicero's works other than the Letters to Atticus. The
reason is that the clustering algorithm greatly prefers to put works by
the same author together. This is great, but we're not interested in the
authorship of the Letters. We're interested in how similar it is to
other texts of the same genre. So, Cicero has to go.

The numbers aren't normalized according to the size of their respective
works yet, so I've divided each number by the total number of words in
the given work in the next chunk. I multiplied each by 100,000 too, just
to get numbers that look a little nicer and aren't in scientific
notation.

Finally, we do what I said above, turning the rownames into the title. I
shorten them to 12 characters max.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

#Removed punctuation because PCA showed that it was an "important" variable
final.frame <- subset(final.frame, select = c(-PUNCT, -X, -VERB, -Sing, -Plur))

#Move letters to Atticus to the top
row.one <- final.frame[final.frame$title %in% c("Letters to Atticus"),]
other.rows <- final.frame[final.frame$title %in% c("Letters to Atticus") == FALSE,]
final.frame <- rbind(row.one, other.rows)
rownames(frame) <- 1:length(frame$title) #Replace the rownames with the new correct number
remove(other.rows)
remove(row.one)


#Clean up NAs and filter out Cicero's works that are not the Letters to Atticus
final.frame[is.na(final.frame)] <- 0

#Add a number to make

#CAN GET RID OF THIS LATER WHEN I FIX TITLES
#final.frame$title[46] <- "Apocolocyntosis"
#final.frame <- final.frame[grepl("Horace", final.frame$title) == FALSE,]

#cicero_works_nonatt <- c("M. Tulli Ciceronis Orationes", "Librorum de Re Publica Sex", "M. Tulli Ciceronis\nscripta quae manserunt omnia,\nfasc. 43", "de Natura Deorum", "M. Tullii Ciceronis opera quae supersunt omnia", "Tusculanae Disputationes", "M. Tulli Ciceronis Rhetorica, Tomus II", "De Senectute De Amicitia De Divinatione, With An English Translation", "Epistulae ad Familiares", "Letters to and from Quintus")

# #final.frame <- final.frame[(final.frame$title %in% cicero_works_nonatt) == FALSE,]
# 
# 
# #These values give NA, cause issues
# final.frame[c("Neg", "Rcp", "Art")] <- NULL

for (i in 1:length(final.frame$title)) {
  final.frame$title <- as.character(final.frame$title)
  final.frame$title[i] <- paste(as.character(i), final.frame$title[i])
}

rownames(final.frame) <- final.frame$title
final.frame$title <- lapply(X=final.frame$title, FUN=substr, start=1, stop=16)
rownames(final.frame) <- lapply(X=rownames(final.frame), FUN=substr, start=1, stop=16)

```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Now that we have a viable DF, let's cut it up a little bit. We don't
want the first column, because that's all text. Not every clustering
algorithm needs the DF to be pre-scaled (that is, subtracting the mean
from every value and dividing it by the standard deviation to make the
comparable). However, some do expect the data to be scaled, so lets
create two variables. **df_clust** isn't scaled, and **df_scaled** is.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuXG5kZl9jbHVzdCA8LSBmaW5hbC5mcmFtZVssMjpsZW5ndGgoZmluYWwuZnJhbWUpXVxuI2RmX2NsdXN0IDwtIHNsaWNlX3NhbXBsZShkZl9jbHVzdCwgbj00NSlcblxuZGZfc2NhbGVkIDwtIGFzLmRhdGEuZnJhbWUoc2NhbGUoZGZfY2x1c3QpKVxuYGBgIn0= -->

```r

df_clust <- final.frame[,2:length(final.frame)]
#df_clust <- slice_sample(df_clust, n=45)

df_scaled <- as.data.frame(scale(df_clust))
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


# Analysis

First, to get some of the formatting predefined:


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r
#10 variables to use for analysis. This is based of the work done in the Testing section below
vars <- c("2", "PART", "Imp", "Pres", "Part", "Abl", "Pass", "Fut", "1", "Prs")
df_clust <- df_clust %>% select(all_of(vars))
df_scaled <- df_scaled %>% select(all_of(vars))

#Add this with the + operator to fviz_cluster plots. @param data is the original dataset used for cluster analysis (df_clust in the first case)
cluster_labels <- geom_text_repel(aes(label=rownames(data)), vjust="inward", hjust="inward", max.overlaps = 20, size=3)

#Args for fviz_clust
palette <- c("#2E9FDF", "#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "red","gray")
geom <- "point"
ellipse.type <- "convex"
ggtheme <- theme_bw()

#K-means
k <- 4

#Hcut
hc_func <- "agnes"
hc_method <- "ward"
hc_metric <- "euclidean"
cex <- 0.2

#Text labels for distance diagrams. @param label is usually the rownames of a data.frame
margin_size <- 10
distance_labels <- theme(axis.text = element_text(size=5, vjust = 1), margins = margin(t = margin_size, r = margin_size, b = margin_size, l = margin_size))

#Dendrogram theme
dend_size <- 0.2
dend_theme <- theme(axis.text = element_text(size=dend_size, vjust = 1), margins = margin(t = margin_size, r = margin_size, b = margin_size, l = margin_size))

```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


## K-means clustering

Time to cluster! K-means is a good starting point here. I'll check out
CLARA as well. We'll need to evaluate what's best. Here's the
modifications we can make:

1.  Change which variables we account for.

2.  Change the number of centers (between 3 and 5? But we already have a
    way of visualizing that)

3.  Change the measuring method (i.e. Euclidean

4.  Change the algorithm


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

kmeans_basic_cluster <- kmeans(df_scaled, centers=k, nstart=25)
kmeans_basic_table <- data.frame(kmeans_basic_cluster$size, kmeans_basic_cluster$centers)
kmeans_basic_df <- data.frame(Clusters = kmeans_basic_cluster$cluster, df_clust)

```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Let's do our first visualization. Four clusters seems like a reasonable
amount, looking at the fviz_nbclust results with the wss method.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

#TODO ADD SUMMARIES OF THE ABOVE DATA HERE

#Principal component analysis

#pca <- prcomp(df_scaled)

#summary(pca)

#Visualizations

fviz_nbclust(df_clust, kmeans, method="wss", nstart=50)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbXX0= -->

<img src="data:image/png;base64," />

<!-- rnb-plot-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

cluster_main <- fviz_cluster(kmeans_basic_cluster, 
             data = df_clust, 
             palette = palette, 
             geom = geom,
             ellipse.type = ellipse.type, 
             ggtheme = ggtheme,
             ) + geom_text_repel(aes(label=rownames(df_clust)), vjust="inward", hjust="inward", max.overlaps = 12, size=2)
cluster_main
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbWzEsIlx1MDAxYkcyO1x1MDAxYkgyO1dhcm5pbmdcdTAwMWJoOiBnZ3JlcGVsOiAxOSB1bmxhYmVsZWQgZGF0YSBwb2ludHMgKHRvbyBtYW55IG92ZXJsYXBzKS4gQ29uc2lkZXIgaW5jcmVhc2luZyBtYXgub3ZlcmxhcHNcdTAwMWJnXG4iXV19 -->

<img src="data:image/png;base64," />

<!-- rnb-plot-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiXG48IS0tIHJuYi1zb3VyY2UtYmVnaW4gZXlKa1lYUmhJam9pWUdCZ2NseHVYRzV6ZEhJb2EyMWxZVzV6WDJKaGMybGpYMk5zZFhOMFpYSXBYRzVnWUdBaWZRPT0gLS0+XG5cbmBgYHJcblxuc3RyKGttZWFuc19iYXNpY19jbHVzdGVyKVxuYGBgXG5cbjwhLS0gcm5iLXNvdXJjZS1lbmQgLS0+XG4ifQ== -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuXG5zdHIoa21lYW5zX2Jhc2ljX2NsdXN0ZXIpXG5gYGAifQ== -->

```r

str(kmeans_basic_cluster)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->

```
List of 9
 $ cluster     : int [1:172] 4 3 3 3 3 3 3 3 3 3 ...
 $ centers     : num [1:4, 1:10] 0.314 -0.808 -0.367 1.9 -0.999 ...
  ..- attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2
  .. ..$ : chr [1:4] "1" "2" "3" "4"
  .. ..$ : chr [1:10] "2" "PART" "Imp" "Pres" ...
 $ totss       : num 1710
 $ withinss    : num [1:4] 117 113.8 151.2 56.9
 $ tot.withinss: num 439
 $ betweenss   : num 1271
 $ size        : int [1:4] 39 61 44 28
 $ iter        : int 3
 $ ifault      : int 0
 - attr(*, "class")= chr "kmeans"
```



<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r
distance <- get_dist(df_scaled)
fviz_dist(distance, gradient = list(low = "#00AFBB", mid = "white", high = "#FC4E07")) + distance_labels

ggsave(filename = "Images/Distance plot with main d.f.png", dpi="print")

```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbXX0= -->

<img src="data:image/png;base64," />

<!-- rnb-plot-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


I want to try this graph with the best combo of factors for PCA alone. I
found that c("2", "PART", "Imp", "Pres", "Part", "Abl") could explain a
full 90% of the textual variation with the first two principal
components.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

df_sub <- df_scaled %>% select("2", PART, Imp, Pres, Part, Abl)
df_sub_unscaled <- df_clust %>% select("2", PART, Imp, Pres, Part, Abl)

kmeans.res <- kmeans(df_sub, centers = k, nstart = 50)

fviz_nbclust(df_sub, kmeans, method="wss", nstart = 50)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbXX0= -->

<img src="data:image/png;base64," />

<!-- rnb-plot-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

cluster_main <- fviz_cluster(kmeans.res, 
             data = df_sub, 
             palette = palette, 
             geom = geom,
             ellipse.type = ellipse.type, 
             ggtheme = ggtheme,
             ) + geom_text_repel(aes(label=rownames(df_clust)), vjust="inward", hjust="inward", max.overlaps = 12, size=2)
cluster_main

ggsave(filename = "Images/six_factors.png", dpi="print")

```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-plot-begin eyJoZWlnaHQiOjQwOS43NjUxLCJ3aWR0aCI6NjYzLCJkcGkiOi0xLCJzaXplX2JlaGF2aW9yIjowLCJjb25kaXRpb25zIjpbWzEsIlx1MDAxYkcyO1x1MDAxYkgyO1dhcm5pbmdcdTAwMWJoOiBnZ3JlcGVsOiAxMDYgdW5sYWJlbGVkIGRhdGEgcG9pbnRzICh0b28gbWFueSBvdmVybGFwcykuIENvbnNpZGVyIGluY3JlYXNpbmcgbWF4Lm92ZXJsYXBzXHUwMDFiZ1xuIl1dfQ== -->

<img src="data:image/png;base64," />

<!-- rnb-plot-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Let's take a look at a data frame with the clusters added.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r
km.dataframe <- data.frame(clusters = kmeans.res$cluster, df_sub)
km.dataframe$title <- final.frame$title

km.unscaled <- data.frame(clusters = kmeans.res$cluster, df_sub_unscaled)
km.unscaled$title <- final.frame$title

get_means <- function(INDEX) {
  temp <- select(km.unscaled, -title)
  apply(X=temp[temp$clusters == INDEX,], MARGIN = 2, FUN=mean)
}

cluster_means <- map(unique(km.unscaled$clusters), get_means)
cluster_means
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->

```
[[1]]
  clusters         X2       PART        Imp       Pres       Part        Abl 
   1.00000  250.18782  303.74318   41.28716  907.23358  537.64760 1078.78680 

[[2]]
  clusters         X2       PART        Imp       Pres       Part        Abl 
   2.00000   60.14127  199.22323   10.40872  392.14723  743.12188 1412.73457 

[[3]]
 clusters        X2      PART       Imp      Pres      Part       Abl 
   3.0000  522.5767  123.6006  124.2560 1265.6285  607.5766 1125.2081 

[[4]]
 clusters        X2      PART       Imp      Pres      Part       Abl 
   4.0000 1218.5498  196.8296  216.5851 1776.9405  312.8467  602.8821 
```



<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiXG48IS0tIHJuYi1zb3VyY2UtYmVnaW4gZXlKa1lYUmhJam9pWUdCZ2NseHVjbVZ0YjNabEtHUm1YM04xWWlsY2JuSmxiVzkyWlNocmJXVmhibk11Y21WektWeHVZR0JnSW4wPSAtLT5cblxuYGBgclxucmVtb3ZlKGRmX3N1YilcbnJlbW92ZShrbWVhbnMucmVzKVxuYGBgXG5cbjwhLS0gcm5iLXNvdXJjZS1lbmQgLS0+XG4ifQ== -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxucmVtb3ZlKGRmX3N1YilcbnJlbW92ZShrbWVhbnMucmVzKVxuYGBgIn0= -->

```r
remove(df_sub)
remove(kmeans.res)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


So, we have the cluster means for our parameters now

I commented this out because I'm done with it, but I saved the results
in a data table. Because K-means has an element of randomness, the
results aren't identical every time, but Hartigan-Wong, the default
algorithm, either had the lowest or tied for the lowest total wihin sum
of squares.

+----------+----------+
| a        | t o t    |
| lgorithm | .        |
|          | withinss |
| \<chr\>  |          |
|          | \<dbl\>  |
+:=========+=========:+
| H a r t  | 3988.635 |
| i        |          |
| gan-Wong |          |
+----------+----------+
| Lloyd    | 3989.989 |
+----------+----------+
| Forgy    | 3993.158 |
+----------+----------+
| MacQueen | 3989.989 |
+----------+----------+


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

# 
# algorithms <- c("Hartigan-Wong", "Lloyd", "Forgy", "MacQueen")
# 
# algorithms.tot.withinss <- vector(mode = "double")
# for (i in 1:length(algorithms)) {
#   algorithms.tot.withinss[i] <- kmeans(df_scaled, centers=k, nstart=25, algorithm = algorithms[i])$tot.withinss
# }
# 
# data.frame(algorithm = algorithms, tot.withinss = algorithms.tot.withinss)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


But, let's look at the cluster that contains the letters to Atticus.
Maybe a bar chart showing facets of each?


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

# 
# algorithms <- c("Hartigan-Wong", "Lloyd", "Forgy", "MacQueen")
# 
# algorithms.tot.withinss <- vector(mode = "double")
# for (i in 1:length(algorithms)) {
#   algorithms.tot.withinss[i] <- kmeans(df_scaled, centers=k, nstart=25, algorithm = algorithms[i])$tot.withinss
# }
# 
# data.frame(algorithm = algorithms, tot.withinss = algorithms.tot.withinss)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


2/15/2026: Interesting to note that all POS's tagged as Singular are
higher in Cluster 3, which Att. belongs to. Verbs also look farthest
above the mean in the same cluster, so that is worth investigating as
well. Present tense is by far the most common in Cluster 3, and it only
comes close in cluster 5.

-   Cluster 3 contains only comedy, Sulpicia, and the Letters to Atticus

## CLARA

Clara is another clustering algorithm like K-means, but which

## AGNES

But it might be a good idea to look at other methods of clustering as
well. The graphs above seem to show that K-means gets some well-defined
groupings, but let's test out hcut. I do know that there are many
methods of hclust, so the next step after some more experimentation is
to try that.

We also want to see what creates the tightest clusters. We have a few
parameters we can change:

1.  The number of variables under consideration, like above

2.  The function (hclust, agnes, or diana)

3.  The agglomeration method (ward.D2 is oft-recommended, but it's worth
    seeing what works best)

4.  The metric (I've only seen Euclidean and Manhattan mentioned, I
    don't know which is best)

**Note:** AGNES apparently comes with a value telling us how tight the
clusters are, according to [this
article](https://uc-r.github.io/hc_clustering).


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

#df_clust2 <- df_clust[sample(nrow(df_clust), 80),]
df_clust2 <- df_clust #I've commented out the one above because sometimes I want a sample

hcut_results <- hcut(df_clust2, k=k, hc_func = hc_func, hc_method = hc_method) #k is defined in the first section in Analysis

```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Now, let's do tests to see what the best variables to study are. It
seems that principal component analysis is the way to go for this.

Here are some of the results of looking at PCA. It looks like Person,
Pronouns, Adjectives, Prepositions, Particles, Personal Pronouns,
Tenses, and AUX are very important. The Ablative too might be a good
idea to include

-   Pass one: c("1", "2", "AUX", "Pres", "Part", "Prs") = 0.88916

-   Pass two: c("1", "2", "PROPN", "Fut", "Fin", "Pass") = 0.87284

-   Pass three: c("1", "2", "3", "AUX", "Pres", "Abl") = 0.88998

-   Pass four: c("1", "ADJ", "SCONJ", "Pres", "Pass", "Prs") = 0.87152

-   Pass five: c("1", "ADP", "Fut", "Pres", "Part", "Pass") = 0.88226

-   

    ```         
    c("2", "PART", "Imp", "Pres", "Part", "Abl") = 0.90210
    ```

This last one has the best results, but what if I add a few more? It
doesn't include Pass, Fut., first person, Prs. I'll try it out in the
PCA testing section.

c("2", "PART", "Imp", "Pres", "Part", "Abl", "Pass", "Fut", "1", "Prs")

## Testing

This section is for a variety of tests. I look at which parameters
generate the tightest clusters in this first section, and then I select
random variables and see which combinations give the highest Proportion
of Variance in the first 2 principal components. I use this to try and
identify the best subset of variables to look at. Then, I use PCA again,
this time manually looking at the rotations and removing variables which
have the lowest value. This method actually seems to have less success
than my try-random-variables method, as the best PoV I could explain was
in the 70s%, as opposed to the random selection was in the 80s% or even
around 90%.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiXG48IS0tIHJuYi1zb3VyY2UtYmVnaW4gZXlKa1lYUmhJam9pWUdCZ2NseHVjM1Z0YldGeWVTaHdZMkVwWEc1Y2JtQmdZQ0o5IC0tPlxuXG5gYGByXG5zdW1tYXJ5KHBjYSlcblxuYGBgXG5cbjwhLS0gcm5iLXNvdXJjZS1lbmQgLS0+XG4ifQ== -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuc3VtbWFyeShwY2EpXG5cbmBgYCJ9 -->

```r
summary(pca)

```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiSW1wb3J0YW5jZSBvZiBmaXJzdCBrPTIgKG91dCBvZiAxMCkgY29tcG9uZW50czpcbiAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgUEMxICAgIFBDMlxuU3RhbmRhcmQgZGV2aWF0aW9uICAgICAyLjY3MzAgMS4xMjAwXG5Qcm9wb3J0aW9uIG9mIFZhcmlhbmNlIDAuNzE0NSAwLjEyNTRcbkN1bXVsYXRpdmUgUHJvcG9ydGlvbiAgMC43MTQ1IDAuODM5OVxuIn0= -->

```
Importance of first k=2 (out of 10) components:
                          PC1    PC2
Standard deviation     2.6730 1.1200
Proportion of Variance 0.7145 0.1254
Cumulative Proportion  0.7145 0.8399
```



<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Now that I've read up a bit more in PCA in R, I know that I can actually
see the strength of correlation for each individual variable


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiXG48IS0tIHJuYi1zb3VyY2UtYmVnaW4gZXlKa1lYUmhJam9pWUdCZ2NseHVjM1Z0YldGeWVTaHdZMkVwWEc1Y2JtQmdZQ0o5IC0tPlxuXG5gYGByXG5zdW1tYXJ5KHBjYSlcblxuYGBgXG5cbjwhLS0gcm5iLXNvdXJjZS1lbmQgLS0+XG4ifQ== -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuc3VtbWFyeShwY2EpXG5cbmBgYCJ9 -->

```r
summary(pca)

```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiSW1wb3J0YW5jZSBvZiBmaXJzdCBrPTIgKG91dCBvZiAxMCkgY29tcG9uZW50czpcbiAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgUEMxICAgIFBDMlxuU3RhbmRhcmQgZGV2aWF0aW9uICAgICAyLjY3MzAgMS4xMjAwXG5Qcm9wb3J0aW9uIG9mIFZhcmlhbmNlIDAuNzE0NSAwLjEyNTRcbkN1bXVsYXRpdmUgUHJvcG9ydGlvbiAgMC43MTQ1IDAuODM5OVxuIn0= -->

```
Importance of first k=2 (out of 10) components:
                          PC1    PC2
Standard deviation     2.6730 1.1200
Proportion of Variance 0.7145 0.1254
Cumulative Proportion  0.7145 0.8399
```



<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Given the previous section, let's only remove the following items:

-   VERB

-   CCONJ

-   X

-   NOUN

-   ADJ

-   Sing

-   Plur

-   Act

-   All cases but Abl

-   Degree

-   Neg


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiXG48IS0tIHJuYi1zb3VyY2UtYmVnaW4gZXlKa1lYUmhJam9pWUdCZ2NseHVjM1Z0YldGeWVTaHdZMkVwWEc1Y2JtQmdZQ0o5IC0tPlxuXG5gYGByXG5zdW1tYXJ5KHBjYSlcblxuYGBgXG5cbjwhLS0gcm5iLXNvdXJjZS1lbmQgLS0+XG4ifQ== -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuc3VtbWFyeShwY2EpXG5cbmBgYCJ9 -->

```r
summary(pca)

```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-output-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiSW1wb3J0YW5jZSBvZiBmaXJzdCBrPTIgKG91dCBvZiAxMCkgY29tcG9uZW50czpcbiAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgICAgUEMxICAgIFBDMlxuU3RhbmRhcmQgZGV2aWF0aW9uICAgICAyLjY3MzAgMS4xMjAwXG5Qcm9wb3J0aW9uIG9mIFZhcmlhbmNlIDAuNzE0NSAwLjEyNTRcbkN1bXVsYXRpdmUgUHJvcG9ydGlvbiAgMC43MTQ1IDAuODM5OVxuIn0= -->

```
Importance of first k=2 (out of 10) components:
                          PC1    PC2
Standard deviation     2.6730 1.1200
Proportion of Variance 0.7145 0.1254
Cumulative Proportion  0.7145 0.8399
```



<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


## POS, VERBS, etc.

Note that these analyses are old, older than when I did my tests with
PCA. It seems that the variables I've settled on explain the variance
better than just POS or just Verbs or categories like that.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->



<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Note in the following how the Letters to Atticus are quite far away from
Comedy and Petronius! Unlike the previous, when looking at POS tags.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->



<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->



<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r
scaled_verb <- scale(df_verb)
kmeans_basic_cluster <- kmeans(scaled_verb, centers=5, nstart=20)
kmeans_basic_table <- data.frame(kmeans_basic_cluster$size, kmeans_basic_cluster$centers)
kmeans_basic_df <- data.frame(Clusters = kmeans_basic_cluster$cluster, df_verb)

#TODO ADD SUMMARIES OF THE ABOVE DATA HERE

pca <- prcomp(scaled_verb)

summary(pca)

#Visualizations

choose <- NULL

fviz_nbclust(scaled_verb, kmeans, method="wss", nstart=50)

fviz_cluster(kmeans_basic_cluster, choose.vars = choose, data = df_verb, palette = palette, 
             geom = "point",
             ellipse.type = "convex", 
             ggtheme = theme_bw(),
             ) + geom_text_repel(aes(label=rownames(df_clust)))
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


When looking at Case, it seems that Comedy, Seneca, Petronius, and the
Letters to Atticus cluster together again. It is a strong enough cluster
that they nearly form their own category.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->



<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->



<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r
scaled_case <- scale(df_case)
kmeans_basic_cluster <- kmeans(scaled_case, centers=6, nstart=20)
kmeans_basic_table <- data.frame(kmeans_basic_cluster$size, kmeans_basic_cluster$centers)
kmeans_basic_df <- data.frame(Clusters = kmeans_basic_cluster$cluster, df_case)

#TODO ADD SUMMARIES OF THE ABOVE DATA HERE

pca <- prcomp(scaled_case)

summary(pca)

#Visualizations

choose <- NULL

fviz_nbclust(scaled_case, kmeans, method="wss", nstart=50)

fviz_cluster(kmeans_basic_cluster, choose.vars = choose, data = df_case, palette = c(palette, "brown"), 
             geom = "point",
             ellipse.type = "convex", 
             ggtheme = theme_bw(),
             ) + geom_text(aes(label=rownames(df_clust)))
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


## Notes on Specific Tags

I want to see whether some of my observations from the thesis bear out.
For example, what if we only look at participles? Here are the lowest 25
texts by participle count:


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuZmluYWwuZnJhbWUgJT4lIHNsaWNlX21pbihQYXJ0LCBuPTI1KSAlPiUgc2VsZWN0KHRpdGxlKSAlPiUgcHJpbnQobiA9IDI1KVxuYGBgIn0= -->

```r
final.frame %>% slice_min(Part, n=25) %>% select(title) %>% print(n = 25)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Notice how it's just comedy and the letters to Atticus!!!

But what about other letters? Let's look at Pliny's letters


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuZmluYWwuZnJhbWUgJT4lIFxuICBzbGljZV9taW4oUGFydCwgbj0yMjkpICU+JSBcbiAgc2VsZWN0KHRpdGxlKSAlPiUgXG4gIHByaW50KG4gPSAyMjkpXG4gIFxuYGBgIn0= -->

```r
final.frame %>% 
  slice_min(Part, n=229) %>% 
  select(title) %>% 
  print(n = 229)
  
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


# Misc Tests

I want to understand K-means clustering better, so I'm gonna play around
with it here


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

frame <- data.frame(a=rnorm(10, mean = 10), b=rnorm(10, mean = 20))

scaled <- scale(frame)

scaled

km_result <- kmeans(scaled, centers = 2, nstart = 5)
km_table <- data.frame(km_result$cluster, km_result$centers)

fviz_cluster(km_result, data = scaled, palette = c("gray", "red"), 
             geom = "point",
             ellipse.type = "convex", 
             ggtheme = theme_bw(),
             ) 
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


And some ggplot2 experiments based on the book. Here's some of my
playing around with ch. 8.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r
df <- data.frame(x = 1, y = 3:1, family = c("sans", "serif", "mono"))

df

ggplot(df, aes(x, y)) + 
  geom_text(aes(label = family, family = family))

fviz_cluster(kmeans_basic_cluster, data = df_clust, palette = c("#2E9FDF", "#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "red","gray"), 
             geom = "point",
             ellipse.type = "convex", 
             ggtheme = theme_bw(),
             ) + geom_text_repel(aes(label=rownames(df_clust)), vjust="inward", hjust="inward", check_overlap = TRUE, size=3)

fviz_dend(hcut_results, palette = c("#2E9FDF", "#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "red"), 
          geom = "point",
          ellipse.type = "convex",
          type = "rectangle",
          #type = "phylogenic",
          #repel = TRUE,
          rect = TRUE,
          cex = 0.4,
          ggtheme = theme_bw()
)

fviz_dend(hcut_results, k=4, palette = c("#2E9FDF", "#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "red"), 
          ellipse.type = "convex",
          type = "phylogenic",
          repel = TRUE,
          cex = 0.7,
          ggtheme = theme_bw()
)

```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Some crucial notes on the previous section:

-   Adding the check_overlap argument to geom_text() in fviz_cluster()
    is very useful. It removes overlapping labels, privileging those
    that are higher in the data frame (meaning the letters to Atticus
    are usually visible)

-   Reduce cex to 0.7 for readability

## How K-Means Clustering Works

K-Means clustering itself is relatively simple. The computer has a set
of data, and it wants to determine which observations are closer to each
other than another.

Let's first imagine we're talking about actual distance

| Point | X Coordinate | Y Coordinate |
|-------|--------------|--------------|
| A     | 1            | 0            |
| B     | 2            | 0            |
| C     | 1            | 10           |
| D     | 2            | 10           |
| E     | 3            | 10           |

Let's graph these points:


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuXG5kZiA8LSBkYXRhLmZyYW1lKFguQ29vcmRpbmF0ZSA9IGMoMSwyLDEsMiwzKSwgWS5Db29yZGluYXRlID0gYygwLCAwLCAxMCwgMTAsIDEwKSwgcm93Lm5hbWVzID0gTEVUVEVSU1sxOjVdKVxuXG5nZ3Bsb3QoZGF0YSA9IGRmLCBhZXMoeCA9IFguQ29vcmRpbmF0ZSwgeSA9IFkuQ29vcmRpbmF0ZSkpICsgZ2VvbV9wb2ludCgpXG5gYGAifQ== -->

```r

df <- data.frame(X.Coordinate = c(1,2,1,2,3), Y.Coordinate = c(0, 0, 10, 10, 10), row.names = LETTERS[1:5])

ggplot(data = df, aes(x = X.Coordinate, y = Y.Coordinate)) + geom_point()
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Okay, so we want the computer to recognize that there are two groups of
points close together, the ones at the top and the bottom.

The K-means algorithm lets the computer recognize there are two
clusters.


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxua20gPC0ga21lYW5zKGRmLCBjZW50ZXJzID0gMiwgbnN0YXJ0ID0gNSlcblxuZnZpel9jbHVzdGVyKGttLCBkYXRhID0gZGYpXG5gYGAifQ== -->

```r
km <- kmeans(df, centers = 2, nstart = 5)

fviz_cluster(km, data = df)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


But we're not looking at distances, we're looking at data. However, we
can apply the same method to data that doesn't represent an actual
distance.

Let's say we have a bunch of students, and each take two classes. We
want to sort the students into groups, but how do we do that when they
have two grades in different classes? We could do it by eye, but what if
they were taking 4 classes? 5 classes? How can we objectively sort them
into groups?

Starting with the simplest case, here are some grades:

| Student | English | Math |
|---------|---------|------|
| Sally   | 95      | 89   |
| Susan   | 96      | 72   |
| Sam     | 55      | 78   |
| Shelly  | 76      | 75   |
| Stu     | 40      | 96   |

We can plot these values in space, even though they are


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin  -->

```r

df <- data.frame(English = c(95, 96, 55, 76, 40), Math = c(89, 72, 78, 75, 96), row.names = c("Sally", "Susan", "Sam", "Shelly", "Stu"))

ggplot(df, aes(x = English, y = Math)) + geom_point() + geom_text(aes(label = rownames(df)), nudge_y = -2)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Well, let's do the same clustering algorithm again.

NOTE: I tried it again without scaling, the results are similar, but
different. Now, Stu is matched up with Sam. (The eagle-eyed among you
might notice there's an extra step in here. If you're curious, we needed
to scale the values (subtract the mean and divide by the standard
deviation) so that they're comparable with each other.)


<!-- rnb-text-end -->


<!-- rnb-chunk-begin -->


<!-- rnb-output-begin  -->


<!-- rnb-source-begin eyJkYXRhIjoiYGBgclxuXG4jc2NhbGVkX2RmID0gc2NhbGUoZGYpXG5cbmttIDwtIGttZWFucyhkZiwgY2VudGVycyA9IDIsIG5zdGFydCA9IDIpXG5cbmZ2aXpfY2x1c3RlcihrbSwgZGF0YSA9IGRmKVxuYGBgIn0= -->

```r

#scaled_df = scale(df)

km <- kmeans(df, centers = 2, nstart = 2)

fviz_cluster(km, data = df)
```

<!-- rnb-source-end -->


<!-- rnb-output-end -->

<!-- rnb-chunk-end -->


<!-- rnb-text-begin -->


Notice how the other students are similar to each other because they're
scores in their two classes are pretty close together. Stu, however, has
one high and one low value (he really doesn't like English), so he's
very different from the others.

R is great because it also handles data with more than two dimensions.
If each student had many classes (Math, English, History, Science,
Music, Art), then we could use principal component analysis to plot
differences between these datasets in 2D space. That way, K-means works
even for large datasets.

<!-- rnb-text-end -->
